video.title: MOUSE: P.I. For Hire | Fully Ramblomatic

captions.text: Boy, it must have been difficult living in  the 1930s with all that financial depression,
rising fascism, and specter of looming  global conflict. How fortunate we are
to be living in 2026, where all we need worry  about is financial depression, rising fascism,
specter of looming global conflict, and  if Toby's getting sent home this week
on Great British Bake Off. But it's thanks  to a nostalgia for that era sparked by the
dribbling out of shit like Steamboat Willy into  the public domain—I know you won't believe me,
but I genuinely mistyped that as "Steam Sale  Willy" in the first draft—that we now have Mouse:
P.I. For Hire, a black and white cartoon portrayal  of prewar society during the rise of fascism where
everyone's a mouse. And if you're worried you'll  confuse it with Art Spiegelman's Maus, a black and
white cartoon portrayal of prewar society during  the rise of fascism where everyone's a mouse,
just remember that Mouse P.I. is the much less  harrowing one, containing only family-friendly
concepts like murder, government corruption,  racism, and gunning down entire Sylvanian Family
playsets worth of anthropomorphized rodents.  Mouse P.I. is self-evidently an unashamedly
generic 1930s private eye noir story in which  we play as great mouse detective Jack Pepper.
It's a pun on pepper jack, you see, and take  a moment to appreciate that because that's
officially as sophisticated as the cheese  references get. Sometimes, it uses the word
"cheddar" as slang for money, and most of the  rest of the time, they just replace a random
noun with something cheese related in a way that  isn't even within earshot of being a pun. Like,
instead of "bootleggers," it's "cheeseleggers."  What? That doesn't even start with a B! What about
Brielegger? Okay, not that. Pirate's Bootylegger;  that's cheese flavored. Maybe this is harder than
I thought. Which is probably why, for all its  cartoonish tone and appearance, I didn't find
Mouse V.D. terribly funny. Jack's investigation  into a couple of missing persons leads him down
the rabbit hole to conspiracy, corruption, and  weird backwoods cults, and a lot of it's told
fairly straight, in stark contrast to everyone's  limbs. There are cartoony concepts, boss fights
against mad German scientists, angry opera singers  and big crocodiles with miniguns, but not much in
the way of actual jokes. You meet a magician's  assistant lady and she's been sawn in half with
her legs in a different chair to the rest of her,  which I guess is a joke, but it's not presented
as one. It's just sorta there. Like a custard pie  that doesn't get thrown but is instead presented
for discussion before a panel of commentators.  "Don't you mean commen-cheddars?" NO.
Mouse B.O. is first and foremost a first-ferson  footer. Don't fall into the trap of assuming
a game called Mouse P.I. about a detective  would be a game about being a detective. Oh,
it pretends. The game opens with Jack arriving  at a crime scene and gathering some clues and
working a couple suspects, but then the  facade drops when a door flies open and
a bunch of burly toughs with clubs and tommy  guns burst in, deploying phrases like, "Oh,
a woise goy, eh?" and we have to shoot them all  to death with a gun for no adequately established
reason. And that very much sets the tone for  things. You do have to go back to your cliche
'30s private eye office between every mission  to piece together the clues you've gathered
on your red string conspiracy board- "Don't  you mean cheese string conspiracy board?" No,
I don't, and stop interrupting. But all that this  actually entails on your part is clicking on all
the clues one by one until Jack says, "Right,  now I know exactly where to go next to gun
down the next three busloads of random dudes."  So let's talk about the shooting. It's fine;
it's a classic-style boomer shooter with  lovingly hand-animated billboard sprite enemies,
with a focus on mobility and wrangling groups.  There's a nice kick to the feel of the guns—and
indeed the kick—and the visual style adds  a lot to that: weapons squash and stretch
all over the place as you cram ammo into them  like you're feeding bees to a horny ferret.
But while massacring a roomful of Max Fleischer  rejects with a tommy gun apparently made from
licorice and processed meat or kicking a Goofy  analog into a stun state before shotgunning him
in the nads in a desperate attempt to delete Goof  Troop from continuity is entertaining in a vacuum,
Mouse P.B.S. doesn't really evolve past  that. Besides the gimmicky boss fights,
it's for the most part waves of the same dudes  piling in through doors and eating shotgun
granola for breakfast washed down with tommy  gun grapefruit juice. Again, the game does try:
a whole range of quirky guns unlock over time, the  best of them being a chemical gun that basically
shoots the dip out of Who Framed Roger Rabbit,  but then there's a rocket launcher that handles
like a Cybertruck ram raiding a cement works and  has the ammo capacity of a cucumber sandwich,
a freeze gun that necessitates running up  to the dudes and kicking them once frozen,
and a rather confusing gun powered by a  living brain that makes people's heads
explode and is reloaded by showing it a trophy  or something? All of which I tried, thought,
"Yep, that's a gun alright," and then went  straight back to using the shotgun and tommy
gun for everything. Which are reloaded the  normal way and don't necessitate running up
and kicking dudes but also don't prevent me  from choosing to do so at my own discretion.
So the gameplay of this game modelled on classic  2D animation feels, if you'll forgive the gag,
a bit flat and probably goes on too long past the  point that it, if you'll forgive basically the
same gag, wears thin. It's clear that the game's  pretty much gone all in on its visual design as
the selling point. The hand drawn animation's  undeniably impressive, and I like how it evokes
that thing with old cartoons where you can always  tell which bowling ball is the one Tom and Jerry
are about to concuss each other with because it's  the only one that's cel shaded; turns out, this is
also useful in a video game for differentiating  important elements from the background,
doubly important in a monochrome game where the  requisite explodey barrels can't be regulation
red. But let's stop fanning our scrotums from  the peripheries and get our sack skin right in
the camera lens. The inevitable comparison here  is to Cuphead, and this is no Cuphead. At best,
it's Spoonbrow. Cuphead's visuals are full of  life and variety and expression, and that's not
what I'm seeing in Mouse T.M.I. Jack Pepper isn't  expressive. He's only got one facial expression,
and it's "feeling very angry because he's not  yet successfully chewed through his own lips."
Still, I guess it worked for Popeye. As for  variety, half the characters look the same,
which gets confusing if you're trying to follow  the inevitably convoluted red string conspiracy
plot. The thing about Cuphead is that it  never once didn't look exactly like an
old-style rubber hose cartoon come to life. The  gameplay elements blended in like Melania Trump
in a waxwork museum. Mouse Y.M.C.A. in contrast  only ever looks like a billboard sprite shooter,
with all the visual awkwardness that entails:  characters sliding around the environments like
Subbuteo players, becoming paper cutouts when  viewed from above and always turning to face
you like a bunch of creepy orphan ghosts. Oh  goodness, I'd better wrap this up; I'm late
for my hose-straightening appointment. There's fun  to be had in Mouse A.S.P.C.A.—some of that classic
adrenaline-pumping boomer shooter mayhem in a  unique setting—but in all the things it attempts,
it just doesn't quite get there for me. The  cathartic action wears out its welcome. The
comedy doesn't quite land. The visuals don't quite  fit the gameplay. So frankly, I'm filing it under
"wait for a sale." "Don't you mean you're filing  it under Wensleydale?" I will fucking punch you.
